question: How many members will the union type `${A}-${B}` have if type A = 'x' | 'y' | 'z' and type B = '1' | '2'?

options.text: 6 (Cartesian product: 3 * 2)

options.explanation: Template literals create a Cartesian product (all combinations), not a sum. Each A variant is combined with each B variant

options.text: 3 (takes the maximum of the two unions)
options.explanation: TypeScript doesn't take the maximum — it creates all possible combinations: 'x-1', 'x-2', 'y-1', 'y-2', 'z-1', 'z-2'

options.text: 2 (takes the minimum of the two unions)
options.explanation: Template literal types always create the full Cartesian product of all union members

options.text: 'Hello' | 'World' — Capitalize distributes over the union
'Hello' — applies only to the first member
options.explanation: Built-in string manipulation types distribute over union types, applying to each member separately

options.text: 'HELLO' | 'WORLD' — converts the entire string to uppercase
options.explanation: Capitalize only capitalizes the first letter. Use Uppercase for converting the entire string

options.text: string — loses the literal type
options.explanation: Capitalize preserves the literal type, transforming only the first character of each union member

options.text: Creates a type accepting any string of the format '<number>px'
Compile error — number cannot be used in template literals
options.explanation: TypeScript allows number, string, boolean, bigint, and null/undefined in template literal types. number creates a pattern for any number

options.text: Creates a union of all possible numbers with the px suffix
options.explanation: TypeScript doesn't enumerate all numbers — number in a template literal creates a pattern, not a finite union

options.text: Creates type string
options.explanation: The type preserves the template structure. '100px' will be compatible, but '100' or 'px' won't be

question: How does infer work in a template literal: S extends `${infer Head}-${infer Tail}` ? [Head, Tail] : [S]?

options.text: Head captures everything before the first '-', Tail captures everything after the first '-'
Head captures the first character, Tail captures the last
options.explanation: infer in template literals captures substrings relative to the delimiter. Head = everything before the first '-', Tail = everything after

options.text: Head and Tail split the string in half
options.explanation: The split happens at the delimiter '-', not at a position. For 'a-b-c': Head = 'a', Tail = 'b-c'

options.text: Error — you can't use two infers in a template literal
options.explanation: Multiple infers can be used in a template literal. Each captures its portion of the string

question: Why do you need to write K & string when using Capitalize in a mapped type with keyof?

options.text: keyof T can contain number and symbol, but Capitalize only works with string
It's a TypeScript bug that will be fixed in future versions
options.explanation: This is not a bug but a consequence of object keys being string, number, or symbol. The intersection K & string filters out non-string keys

options.text: Without & string, Capitalize returns never
options.explanation: Without & string you get a compile error (Type 'K' does not satisfy the constraint 'string'), not never

options.text: & string converts number keys to string keys
options.explanation: & string doesn't convert, it intersects. Numeric keys (number) don't intersect with string, so they are simply excluded from the result
